class_schedule.topic: Why Theory Matters in International Relations
learning_objectives: Explain why theoretical frameworks are necessary for understanding world politics
Distinguish between paradigms, theories, and models
Identify the major IR paradigms: realism, liberalism, constructivism
readings: Mingst & Arreguin-Toft, Essentials of International Relations Ch. 1
Walt, 'International Relations: One World, Many Theories'

lecture_notes.introduction: Theory is a lens that determines what we see and what we ignore. In IR, different theories lead to fundamentally different policy prescriptions.
key_concepts: Theory as a simplifying lens for complex reality
Paradigms: overarching worldviews that frame research
The great debates in IR: idealism vs. realism, behavioralism, post-positivism
Ontology and epistemology in IR theory
Theory-policy connection: how theory shapes foreign policy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the same event (Russia-Ukraine conflict) through realist, liberal, and constructivist lenses

review.value: A broad worldview or framework of assumptions that shapes how scholars define problems, select variables, and interpret evidence
Foundational disagreements in IR: idealism vs. realism (1930s-40s), traditionalism vs. behavioralism (1960s), positivism vs. post-positivism (1980s-90s)

learning_objectives: Explain the core assumptions of realism: anarchy, self-help, power
Distinguish between classical realism and structural realism
Apply realist analysis to contemporary security dilemmas
readings: Waltz, Theory of International Politics Ch. 6 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Realism is the oldest and most influential paradigm in IR. It views international politics as a struggle for power in an anarchic world.
key_concepts: Anarchy as the ordering principle of international politics
Classical realism: human nature drives the pursuit of power (Morgenthau)
Structural realism: the system's anarchic structure causes competition (Waltz)
The security dilemma: defensive measures perceived as threats
Balance of power as a systemic tendency
lecture_notes.demonstration: Security dilemma simulation: two states choosing between arming and disarming
setup_requirements: Historical case studies of arms races

review.value: When one state's efforts to increase its security (e.g., military buildup) make other states feel less secure, triggering a spiral of competition
Waltz's theory that the anarchic structure of the international system, not human nature, explains why states compete for power and security

learning_objectives: Explain the liberal paradigm's core assumptions: cooperation, institutions, interdependence
Distinguish between commercial liberalism, democratic peace theory, and neoliberal institutionalism
Evaluate the conditions under which international cooperation succeeds or fails
readings: Doyle, 'Kant, Liberal Legacies, and Foreign Affairs'

lecture_notes.introduction: Liberalism offers a fundamentally different vision: cooperation is possible, institutions matter, and democracies behave differently from autocracies.
key_concepts: Commercial liberalism: trade promotes peace
Democratic peace theory: democracies rarely fight each other
Neoliberal institutionalism: institutions reduce transaction costs and enable cooperation
Complex interdependence: multiple channels, no hierarchy of issues
Absolute versus relative gains in cooperation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing the European Union as a test case for liberal IR theory
setup_requirements: Trade and conflict data for EU member states

review.value: The empirical observation and theoretical claim that liberal democracies rarely go to war with each other, explained by shared norms, institutional constraints, and economic ties
Keohane's argument that international institutions facilitate cooperation by providing information, reducing transaction costs, and creating reciprocity expectations, even under anarchy

learning_objectives: Explain constructivism's emphasis on ideas, norms, and identity
Survey critical approaches: feminism, postcolonialism, Marxism
readings: Wendt, 'Anarchy Is What States Make of It'

lecture_notes.introduction: Constructivism challenges the materialist assumptions of realism and liberalism by arguing that the social world is constructed through shared ideas and practices.
key_concepts: Constructivism: anarchy is socially constructed, not given
Norms, identities, and interests are mutually constituted
Norm life cycle: emergence, cascade, internalization
Feminist IR: gender as a category of analysis
Postcolonial IR: challenging Eurocentric assumptions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Tracing the norm against landmines from advocacy to the Ottawa Treaty (1997)

review.value: Wendt's argument that 'anarchy is what states make of it' -- the meaning of anarchy depends on shared ideas, and states can construct cooperative or conflictual relationships
Finnemore and Sikkink's model: norms emerge through advocacy, cascade as a tipping point of states adopts them, and internalize as taken-for-granted

class_schedule.topic: Power, Hegemony, and Power Transition Theory
learning_objectives: Analyze different dimensions of power: hard, soft, smart, structural
Explain hegemonic stability theory and its critics
Evaluate power transition theory and the Thucydides Trap
readings: Nye, The Future of Power Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Power is the central concept in IR, yet it is deeply contested. How we define and measure power shapes our understanding of global politics.
key_concepts: Hard power: military and economic coercion
Soft power: attraction through culture, values, and legitimacy
Structural power: the ability to shape the rules of the game
Hegemonic stability theory: a dominant state provides public goods
Power transition theory: war risk increases when a rising power approaches parity with a declining hegemon
lecture_notes.demonstration: Applying the Thucydides Trap framework to US-China relations

review.value: Nye's concept: the ability to achieve objectives through attraction rather than coercion, derived from a country's culture, political values, and foreign policy legitimacy
Allison's concept: the structural tension that arises when a rising power threatens to displace an established power, historically resulting in war 12 of 16 times

learning_objectives: Apply multiple theoretical perspectives to a contemporary issue
Evaluate the explanatory power of competing theories
Develop a theoretically informed policy recommendation

lecture_notes.introduction: Theory is only useful if it illuminates real-world problems. In this final session, we apply our theoretical toolkit to the most pressing contemporary challenges.
key_concepts: Climate change through IR theory lenses
Great power competition: realist, liberal, and constructivist explanations
Non-state actors and the limits of state-centric theory
Technology and IR: cyberspace, AI, and new domains of competition
Theoretical pluralism: using multiple lenses for richer analysis
lecture_notes.demonstration: Panel debate: teams apply realist, liberal, and constructivist perspectives to propose strategies for managing US-China competition

review.value: The practice of drawing on multiple theoretical perspectives to analyze a problem, recognizing that no single theory captures all dimensions of complex international issues
The relationship between academic theory and real-world policy: realism leads to balance of power strategies, liberalism to institution-building, constructivism to norm promotion

quizzes.topic: Realism and Liberalism in International Relations

questions.question: Compare and contrast Morgenthau's classical realism with Waltz's structural realism. Where do they agree, and what is the fundamental difference in their explanation of why states seek power?
questions.answer: Both agree that the international system is anarchic, states are the primary actors, and power is central to international politics. The fundamental difference is in causation: Morgenthau (classical realism) locates the drive for power in human nature -- leaders are inherently power-seeking. Waltz (structural realism) locates it in the structure of the international system -- anarchy forces even status-quo states to compete for security regardless of their leaders' intentions. Waltz's theory is systemic (structure explains behavior), while Morgenthau's is unit-level (human nature explains behavior).

questions.question: What is the security dilemma? Explain how it can lead to an arms race even when both states prefer peace.
questions.answer: The security dilemma occurs when one state's efforts to increase its own security (e.g., building up its military) are perceived as threatening by other states, who then respond with their own military buildup. Even when both states genuinely prefer peace, neither can be certain of the other's intentions under anarchy. Each state, acting defensively, appears offensive to the other. The result is a spiral of mutual armament that leaves both states less secure and poorer than if they had cooperated, a tragic outcome driven by uncertainty and the absence of a central authority to guarantee agreements.

questions.question: Explain Wendt's claim that 'anarchy is what states make of it.' How does this challenge the realist assumption that anarchy necessarily leads to self-help and competition?
questions.answer: Wendt argues that anarchy does not have a single, fixed meaning. Realists treat anarchy as automatically producing self-help and competition, but Wendt contends that the meaning of anarchy depends on the shared ideas and identities states bring to their interactions. States that view each other as rivals (Hobbesian culture) will behave competitively, but states that view each other as partners (Kantian culture) can cooperate under the same structural condition of anarchy. The US and Canada exist under anarchy but do not arm against each other, while the US and North Korea do. The difference is not structure but socially constructed identities and expectations.

rubric.key: Clearly states how it challenges realism

questions.question: Distinguish between hard power and soft power. Give one example of each and explain why soft power alone is insufficient for a state's foreign policy.
questions.answer: Hard power is the ability to coerce others through military force or economic sanctions (e.g., US military deployments to deter aggression). Soft power is the ability to attract and persuade others through culture, values, and policy legitimacy (e.g., South Korea's cultural exports -- K-pop, film -- enhancing its global image and influence). Soft power alone is insufficient because it depends on voluntary attraction: when vital interests are at stake or adversaries are not susceptible to cultural appeal, coercive capability remains necessary. A state with only soft power cannot deter an invasion or enforce sanctions.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both types of power
Explains the limitation of soft power alone

major_assignments.title: Theoretical Analysis of an International Crisis
major_assignments.description: Select a major international crisis (historical or ongoing) and analyze it through at least two competing theoretical perspectives. Evaluate which theory best explains the crisis and what policy implications follow from each theoretical interpretation.
objectives: Apply IR theories to a real-world case
Compare the explanatory power of competing paradigms
Demonstrate critical evaluation of theoretical frameworks

criteria: Theories applied systematically to the case
Key concepts used correctly and precisely
Multiple perspectives on the crisis considered
Honest assessment of each theory's strengths and limits
Reasoned judgment about which theory explains more
Acknowledgment of what each theory misses
Rigorous application of at least two IR theories to a real crisis
Strong evidence base and thorough case analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes testing comprehension of theoretical readings and concepts

seminar_participation.description: Active engagement in theoretical discussions and debate exercises

major_assignments.description: Theoretical crisis analysis paper and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Essay exam requiring theoretical analysis and comparison

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam integrating all major IR theories

course_policies.attendance: Attendance and preparation are required for seminar-style discussion. More than 2 unexcused absences will reduce the participation grade by 50%.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late papers lose 5% per day. The policy brief cannot be submitted late as it is discussed in seminar.
course_policies.collaboration: Theoretical debate preparation may be collaborative. All written analysis must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Proper attribution of all ideas is essential in theoretical writing. Plagiarism results in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to IR journals through university library, JSTOR, and course reserves.
